# Transcrição: Look_Beyond_Surface-Level_Results

## [00:00 - 03:01]

This is the Pubcast Shot. I'm John Loomer. Let's talk about the many layers of results. Let's go. One of the many mistakes I see advertisers make is related to the interpretation of results. They do one of two things. First, they might oversimplify the results to focus only on the number of conversions and cost per conversion. Second, they might get lost in all the secondary metrics like CPM, CPC, CTR, and everything else that might contribute to results but aren't necessarily all that important. The conversion itself is absolutely most important, but there are layers to those conversions. Let me explain. First, make sure that the Compare Attribution Settings feature is a regular part of your routine. Do not take your conversion results at face value. How many of your conversions are one-day view? If it's a high percentage, your results mean a whole lot less. How many are seven-day click versus one-day click? While both are meaningful, one-day click is a clear indication of being driven by your ad, especially if you're not selling a high-priced product, seven-day click could get help from remarketing and other platforms. You can also uncover more conversions with 28-day click. This is especially useful when running ads for leads and you have a sales outreach team that starts immediately. You can get a sense of how many of those leads ended up buying from you within 28 days by extending the window. Now, what's the difference between first conversion and all conversions? Meta reports all conversions by default, but that can be an inflated view. One person could perform multiple conversions, and this often leads to mismatched reporting. You can get a clear sense of unique customers by using first conversion reporting. Second, get to know your breakdown options. Breakdown by audience segments to see how budget and results are impacted by remarketing. Breakdown age or gender to see if performance is impacted by these demographics. And you might even spot a problem in distribution if you're able to get cheap actions from a certain demographic group. Breakdown by placement to see where people are seeing your ads. Breakdown by dynamic creative element to view whether people are seeing your video or static image version from a single ad. Or breakdown by text or headline when you use multiple text options. Third, know the impact of conversion value. Your results don't necessarily reflect the purchase of the specific product you're selling in your ad. As long as it's a purchase, it will be reported. So the value may not match up with what you expect.

## [03:01 - 05:45]

View total value and return on ad spend and make sure these aren't inflated by view through conversions. Next, what are you seeing on the back end? Is meta overreporting or underreporting what is actually happening? Are your events set up properly and firing on the right pages? Are they deduplicated? If you're running ads for lead generation, what do those leads do? Are you able to contact them? What is your conversion rate? If you're getting low quality leads, what makes them low quality? Is it a problem with your sales process or is it something that you can control with your ads? So here's the bottom of the glass. Measuring performance isn't as simple as looking at a couple of metrics and deciding it's good or bad. There are many layers to this analysis. Now you don't need to paralyze yourself with going through every possible layer, but understand that these layers exist. Do that extra digging when that digging is needed. Be a sophisticated advertiser who looks beyond the surface level results. Be curious and ask questions. Don't trust results that seem too good to be true or too impossibly bad. Keep digging. One of the useful tools I mentioned at the top is compare attribution settings. I wrote a blog post about my four favorite ways to get better reporting with this tool. Read it at johnloomer.com.
